description:
You are acting as a QA engineer who is preparing a small, reproducible data-set for automated regression tests.  
    In /home/user/project/test_data there is already a CSV file named raw_metrics.csv that contains module-level test-execution data.  
    Your job is to convert that CSV into two JSON files and to leave a very small textual audit log.

    1. Read the file /home/user/project/test_data/raw_metrics.csv.  
       It always contains exactly four comma-separated columns, in this order:  
           test_case, module, status, execution_time  
       The first line is the header. The rest of the file contains one data row per test case.

    2. Produce a new file called /home/user/project/test_data/raw_metrics.json with *one* JSON array that holds an object for every data row.  
       All keys must be strings; execution_time must be a JSON number (NOT a string).  
       Do NOT prettify or indent the file—write the smallest valid JSON possible (one very long line, no embedded new-lines or spaces).  
       Example (spacing added here only for readability; your file must be compact):  
         [
           {"test_case":"TC001","module":"login","status":"pass","execution_time":2.1},
           {"test_case":"TC002","module":"signup","status":"fail","execution_time":3.4},
           …
         ]

    3. Create a second file called /home/user/project/test_data/summary.json with the following *exact* structure (four keys, the order shown below, no extra whitespace, immediately followed by a trailing newline character):  
         {"total":INT,"passed":INT,"failed":INT,"average_execution_time":FLOAT}\n  
       • total  – total number of rows in raw_metrics.csv  
       • passed – rows whose status column is the string “pass” (case-sensitive)  
       • failed – rows whose status column is the string “fail” (case-sensitive)  
       • average_execution_time – arithmetic mean of the execution_time column rounded to one decimal place

       Make sure that the numbers are written as JSON numbers (no quotes) and that the key order is exactly as shown.

    4. Append a single-line audit log entry to /home/user/project/test_data/conversion.log.  
       The line must be literally:
           CONVERSION_COMPLETED
       followed by a UNIX newline (line-feed, \n).  
       Do not write anything else to the log—no timestamps, no extra spaces.

    The starter files, new files, and their exact byte-for-byte contents will be checked by an automated script, so observe the required format precisely.  
    You can solve this entire task in 3–4 reasonable shell commands, but you are free to approach it however you like.
truth:
<!-- File that already exists before the task starts -->
    <file path="/home/user/project/test_data/raw_metrics.csv"><![CDATA[test_case,module,status,execution_time
TC001,login,pass,2.1
TC002,signup,fail,3.4
TC003,logout,pass,1.7
]]></file>

    <!-- Files that must exist after the agent finishes -->
    <file path="/home/user/project/test_data/raw_metrics.json"><![CDATA[[{"test_case":"TC001","module":"login","status":"pass","execution_time":2.1},{"test_case":"TC002","module":"signup","status":"fail","execution_time":3.4},{"test_case":"TC003","module":"logout","status":"pass","execution_time":1.7}]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/project/test_data/summary.json"><![CDATA[{"total":3,"passed":2,"failed":1,"average_execution_time":2.4}
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/project/test_data/conversion.log"><![CDATA[CONVERSION_COMPLETED
]]></file>
